items.question: Who uses data augmentation in ML projects?
items.answer: ML engineers and data scientists training supervised models on limited labeled data — especially in vision, NLP, and imbalanced tabular tasks — use augmentation to reduce overfitting before spending on new annotations.

items.question: What is data augmentation?
items.answer: Data augmentation creates additional training views by applying label-preserving transforms to existing examples — flips and crops for images, paraphrase or synonym swap for text, noise or SMOTE-style synthesis for tabular data — increasing diversity without new labels.

items.question: When should you add data augmentation?
items.answer: Add augmentation when validation error exceeds training error (overfitting) on a small or repetitive dataset and learning curves suggest variance limits generalization — after confirming transforms preserve labels for your task.

items.question: Where does augmentation fit in the pipeline?
items.answer: After cleaning and splitting data, augmentation runs in the training loop (on-the-fly per batch) or as an offline expand step; validation and test use fixed preprocessing without random heavy transforms.

items.question: Why does augmentation act as regularization?
items.answer: It exposes the model to perturbed inputs with the same label, discouraging memorization of exact training rows and encouraging invariances that match harmless real-world variation — reducing variance and the train–validation gap.

items.question: How do you augment images vs text vs tabular data?
items.answer: Images: random flip, crop, color jitter. Text: synonym replacement, back-translation, light paraphrase with label checks. Tabular: small Gaussian noise on numeric features; SMOTE for minority oversampling when classes are imbalanced — without applying SMOTE to test data.

items.question: Data augmentation vs collecting more real labeled data — when is each preferred?
items.answer: Augmentation is cheaper and fast when transforms simulate real variation and labels stay valid. More real data is preferred when deployment scenarios are absent from the corpus or transforms cannot represent shift — augmentation complements but does not fully replace new labels.

items.question: On-the-fly vs offline augmentation — what is the tradeoff?
items.answer: On-the-fly applies random transforms each epoch (less storage, fresh diversity every pass) but adds CPU/GPU load per batch. Offline pre-generates copies (higher disk use, fixed set) but can speed epoch IO when transforms are expensive.

items.question: Image flips vs text paraphrase — what differs in validation risk?
items.answer: Image flips are easy to audit visually for label preservation on many classification tasks. Text paraphrase can silently change sentiment or entities — requires spot-checking or constraints so the label remains correct.

items.question: Cause: small image dataset, flexible CNN, no augmentation. Effect?
items.answer: Training accuracy rises while validation accuracy lags — overfitting to exact training pixels; adding label-preserving flips and crops often narrows the gap.

items.question: Cause: horizontal flip enabled on digit recognition where 6 and 9 differ by orientation. Effect?
items.answer: Labels become inconsistent under transform — validation accuracy drops and the model learns incorrect invariances; disable or restrict rotation/flip.
